name: Ehecatl
description: Ehecatl, whose name translates to "Wind" in Nahuatl, is a prominent deity within the Mesoamerican pantheon, particularly revered by the cultures of central Mexico, including the Aztec. He is most commonly recognized as the wind god, embodying the vital force that animates the cosmos. Ehecatl is often depicted as a feathered serpent, a visual representation connecting him to Quetzalcoatl, with whom he is frequently associated, sometimes even considered an aspect or manifestation. His influence extends beyond mere atmospheric phenomena; he is viewed as a harbinger of rain, vital for agriculture, and a cleanser who sweeps away impurities. He is also considered a god of creation, responsible for blowing life into the newly formed sun and humanity. Temples dedicated to Ehecatl were often round, designed to minimize wind resistance, and his worship involved offerings and rituals intended to appease and harness his powerful force. His presence underscores the importance of wind as a life-giving and transformative element in the Mesoamerican worldview.
subtitle: The Wind

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: In the Aztec creation myth, after the previous suns had been destroyed, the gods gathered to create the Fifth Sun. Two gods, Nanahuatzin and Tecciztecatl, volunteered to sacrifice themselves. However, only Nanahuatzin bravely leaped into the fire, becoming the sun. Tecciztecatl hesitated but eventually followed, becoming the moon. The sun was motionless, and Ehecatl was called upon to blow it into motion, giving it life and allowing it to traverse the sky, thus beginning the current era. This myth highlights Ehecatl's crucial role in the ongoing creation and maintenance of the world.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Ehecatl and Mayahuel
keyMyths.description: Ehecatl, desiring to be with the goddess Mayahuel, who was kept imprisoned by her grandmother, flew down to earth as wind and uprooted the tree where she was held. He carried her away to the heavens, where they became intertwined as a double-branched tree. When her grandmother discovered their escape, she tore the tree apart, scattering the branches. Mayahuel's branch became the first maguey plant, from which pulque is made, linking Ehecatl to fertility and the gift of sustenance.
keyMyths.source: Bierhorst, John. *Mythology of Mexico and Central America*.

keyMyths.title: Ehecatl as a Cleansing Force
keyMyths.description: Ehecatl is believed to be a powerful force for purification, sweeping away evil and disease. Before the arrival of Tlaloc, the rain god, Ehecatl would clear the skies, removing any lingering malevolent influences that might contaminate the rain. This act ensured that the rain would be beneficial and nourishing to the earth, promoting growth and prosperity. This emphasizes Ehecatl's role in maintaining cosmic balance and protecting humanity from harm.
keyMyths.source: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube. *An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya*.
